MASTER MOTION CALENDAR ON FEB-11-2021 IN 206, DEFENDANTS NOB HILL MASONIC CENTER, INC. AND CALIFORNIA MASONIC MEMORIAL TEMPLE'S MOTION TO CONTINUE TRIAL DATE WAS HEARD. APPEARANCES VIA COURTCALL BY LOREN SCHWARTZ, ESQ. FOR PLAINTIFF; JEFFREY WILSON, ESQ. FOR MOVING PARTY DEFENDANTS; VICTORIA TOWNSEND, ESQ. FOR DEFENDANT ATLAS BAY CITY COMPANY, INC. JUDGE GARRETT WONG RECUSED HIMSELF. MATTER HEARD BY JUDGE SAMUEL FENG. JUDGE FENG MADE A DISCLOSURE THAT HE KNOWS MS. TOWNSEND. NO OBJECTIONS WERE MADE. ARGUED AND THE COURT DENIED THE MOTION. THE TRIAL DATE OF 02/22/21 AT 9:30AM IN DEPT. 206 REMAINS. PARTIES MAY APPEAR IN PERSON OR BY COURTCALL. DISCOVERY CLOSED. JUDGE: SAMUEL K. FENG; MATTER NOT REPORTED (206/SKF)

MOTION TO CONTINUE JURY TRIAL
Off Calendar Feb-11-2021 Text Ruling

Notice Of Motion For Determination Of Good Faith Settloement
Off Calendar Feb-09-2021 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: APPEARANCES VIA ZOOM VIDEOCONFERENCE:
Loren Schwartz (lschwartz@dpllp.com) of Dunn & Panagotacos LLP for Plaintiff, Alexander Quan.
Victoria Townsend (vtownsend@vanlevylaw.com) of Van De Poel, Levy...LLP for Defendant/Moving Party, Atlas Bay City Company, Inc.
Jeffrey P. Wilson (Jeffrey.Wilson01@LibertyMutual.com) of Santana & Vierra for Defendants, Nob Hill Masonic Center Inc., et al.

ARGUED (as fully reported in the reporter's transcript). The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT ATLAS BAY CITY COMPANY, INC.'s Motion For Determination Of Good Faith Settlement is granted. The party asserting the lack of good faith has the burden of proof on that issue (Code Civ. Proc. sec. 877.6(d)). In this case, objectors fail to meet their burden and Atlas has provided substantial evidence that the settlement is in the ballpark. (Tech-Bilt, Inc. v. Woodward-Clyde & Associates (1985) 38 Cal.3d 488; Mattco Forge, Inc. v. Arthur Young & Co. (1995) 38 Cal.App.4th 1337, 1348, 1350 at n.6 [substantial evidence necessary to find that settlement is in good faith].) "The ultimate determinant of good faith is whether the settlement is grossly disproportionate to what a reasonable person at the time of settlement would estimate the settlor's liability to be." (City of Grand Terrace v. Superior Court (1987) 192 Cal.App.3d 1251, 1262.)

In Tech-Bilt, Inc., 38 Cal.3d at 499, the California Supreme Court held the following factors are relevant in evaluating the good faith of a settlement: (1) a rough approximation of plaintiffs' total recovery and the settlor's proportionate liability; (2) the amount paid in settlement; (3) the allocation of settlement proceeds among plaintiffs; (4) a recognition that a settlor should pay less in settlement than he would if he were found liable after a trial; (5) the financial conditions of the settling defendants, as well as the existence of collusion, fraud, or tortious conduct aimed to injure the interests of non-settling defendants; and (6) defendant's settlement figure must not be grossly disproportionate to what a reasonable person, at the time of the settlement, would estimate the settling defendant's liability to be.

Here, plaintiff's damages experts have estimated his economic losses at between $6.4 and $10.3 million, although all defendants anticipate that his likely recovery will be substantially lower. Although objectors Hill Masonic Center, Inc. and the California Masonic Memorial Temple contend that Atlas breached its contractual obligation to apply ground walnut shells to the walkway, that contention is disputed. Moreover, there is evidence that objectors' own accident reconstruction expert has opined that the painted walkway surface had adequate slip resistance and was code compliant; that the Masonic defendants were on notice that the walkway was slippery but failed to place nonskid strips, floor mats, or wet floor cones or signage before plaintiff's fall; and that plaintiff bears some contributory responsibility for his own injuries. Given those considerations and Atlas's agreement to pay all of its available insurance, the $1 million policy limits settlement is in the ballpark. There is no evidence of collusion. The settlement is in good faith given the totality of the circumstances.

Order is signed.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman, Clerk: S. Le, Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR#8600, maria.torreano@gmail.com, Reported. =(302/EPS)
